example: Practicing daily will improve your language skills.
example2: The company is working to improve customer satisfaction.
example3: Her health improved significantly after the treatment.
Explanation: Improve is a fundamental verb for positive change. It can be transitive (you improve something) or intransitive (something improves). It's a broad term covering quality, condition, performance, or value. Improvement can be incremental or dramatic. The desire to improve drives personal development, innovation, and progress in all fields. It's an active, optimistic word. The noun is 'improvement'. It's one of the most common and useful verbs in English, applicable to almost any situation where there is potential for positive growth or enhancement.
commonContext: Personal development, product quality, health, skills
usageTip: Often followed by 'on' (improve on a previous attempt) or 'in' (improve in math).
commonMistake: Using it without a clear sense of what 'better' means; improvement should be measurable or observable.

meaning: To make something better, more efficient, or higher in quality
example: We need to improve the loading speed of our website.
example2: The new update will improve the security of the app.
example3: You can improve your coding skills by practicing every day.
Explanation: To 'improve' is one of the most common goals in IT. Whether it is making code run faster ('Performance Improvement'), making a screen look better ('UI Improvement'), or making a system safer, improvement is a continuous process. In tech, this is often called 'Optimization'. Improvement can be small (tweaking a color) or large (rewriting a whole system). It is a positive word used in almost every professional discussion.
usageTip: Always specify 'what' you are improving (e.g., 'improve efficiency').
commonMistake: Using 'improve' when you actually mean 'fix'; fixing is for broken things, improving is for making good things better.
